sentence: Subjective:
The patient, Jeremy, a 30-year-old male, presents with a 4-month history of lower back pain that began after lifting boxes while moving his son's college dorm.
He reports that the pain is exacerbated by movement and heavy lifting, and is accompanied by occasional numbness and tingling in his right foot that resolved after 1 hour.
He denies any difficulty with urination, bowel movements, or fever.
He reports taking Tylenol and ibuprofen for pain relief, but notes that ibuprofen provides only temporary relief.
He underwent an MRI was recommended at an urgent care visit but did not follow through due to insurance concerns.
He has a past medical history of a total knee replacement in 2020.
He denies any past medical history of other conditions.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any symptoms of bladder or bowel dysfunction, incontinence, or difficulty urinating.
Current medications:
I prescribed Tylenol and ibuprofen for pain relief.
Objective:
Vital signs:
The patient's blood pressure is 117/56 mmHg, heart rate is 68 beats per minute, and oxygen saturation is 95% on room air.
Physical exam:
The patient has bony protuberance of the C5-C6 vertebrae, but no pain to palpation.
The lumbar spine exam reveals no pain to palpation, but decreased flexion and extension of the lower back, a positive straight leg raise, and spasm of the paraspinal muscles of the lumbar spine.
Test results:
The lumbar spine x-ray showed no acute bony abnormality, and no fracture of the back.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an acute disk herniation in the lumbar spine, likely resulting from a 4-month-old injury.
Problem list: Lumbar disk herniation; Acute injury; Chronic back pain.
Plan:
I ordered a lumbar spine MRI to further evaluate the extent of the disk herniation.
I referred the patient to a physical therapist through our health system.
I prescribed meloxicam 15mg once daily to help with pain relief.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient again in 2 weeks to review the MRI results and discuss further treatment options, including the possibility of an epidural steroid injection.
